Title: A chromosome-level genome assembly provides insights into ascorbic acid accumulation and fruit softening in guava (Psidium guajava)

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: AO family expanded in guava; most genes in L-galactose and recycling pathways are fruit-high except two AO members
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: AO family expanded in guava; most genes in L-galactose and recycling pathways are fruit-high except two AO members
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative gene family analysis; RNA-seq expression profiling
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with expanded ascorbate regeneration/redox-related capacity in guava, but not singled out as the primary contributor to high ascorbate accumulation.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AO gene family is expanded in guava relative to comparison species. 2. Most ascorbate pathway genes were fruit-high, but two AO members were exceptions. 3. AO expansion was interpreted as part of the enlarged ascorbate regeneration system in guava.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Expanded family mentioned in regeneration context, but authors emphasize MDHAR rather than DHAR as the main regeneration contributor.

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Only one GalUR member had detectable expression
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Only one GalUR member had detectable expression
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genome annotation and RNA-seq expression profiling
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Minor or limited contribution of the galacturonate pathway to ascorbic acid biosynthesis in guava.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Genes for the galacturonate route were incomplete in guava, with Alase absent. 2. Only one GalUR member had detectable expression. 3. This supports that the galacturonate pathway is not the major source of ascorbic acid in guava.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the paper explicitly contrasts major versus minor pathways.

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Only two MIOX members had detectable expression
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Only two MIOX members had detectable expression
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genome annotation and RNA-seq expression profiling
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Suggests the myo-inositol pathway contributes little to guava fruit ascorbic acid accumulation.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Only two MIOX genes had detectable expression in guava. 2. Other routes besides the L-galactose pathway showed weak expression support. 3. This indicates that the myo-inositol route is not the dominant source of fruit ascorbic acid in guava.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Cell wall remodeling affecting fruit firmness
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Many genes highly expressed in fruits; some increased during ripening
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Many genes highly expressed in fruits; some increased during ripening
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to cell wall degradation and therefore likely fruit softening in guava.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Guava possesses more beta-galactosidase genes than comparison plants. 2. Many cell wall degradation genes had high fruit expression. 3. Some beta-galactosidase genes increased during ripening, supporting a role in fruit softening.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper treats cell wall degradation collectively rather than validating specific family members individually.

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Some genes showed increasingly higher expression during fruit ripening
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Some genes showed increasingly higher expression during fruit ripening
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genome annotation; RNA-seq expression profiling; linkage to prior pectin measurements in literature
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely contributes to pectin degradation during guava fruit softening.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Polygalacturonase genes are part of the 192 cell-wall-degradation genes identified in guava. 2. Some polygalacturonase genes increased in expression during fruit ripening. 3. These data support a role for cell wall degradation in guava softening.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No single PG gene was designated as the principal causal gene.

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Some genes showed increasingly higher expression during fruit ripening
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Some genes showed increasingly higher expression during fruit ripening
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports cell wall degradation associated with guava fruit softening.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Pectate lyase genes were identified among guava cell wall degradation genes. 2. Some pectate lyase genes increased during fruit ripening. 3. This supports a contribution of pectin disassembly to fruit softening.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms direct pathway intermediate/precursor of target product
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant/vitamin C for plant redox homeostasis and human nutrition
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in fruits; positively correlated with fruit development; also high in stem
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed in fruits; positively correlated with fruit development; also high in stem
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genome annotation; RNA-seq expression profiling across tissues and fruit stages; WGCNA-metabolite correlation with ascorbic acid content; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports ascorbic acid accumulation during guava fruit development as part of the major L-galactose biosynthetic route.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GGP was identified in the guava genome as a component of the complete L-galactose pathway. 2. Downstream biosynthetic genes including GGP showed expression positively correlated with fruit ascorbic acid content. 3. A key GGP gene was among six WGCNA-linked genes associated with ascorbic acid accumulation, supporting the L-galactose pathway as the major route in guava.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control point suggested by inclusion among six key WGCNA genes; classical rate-limiting status not directly established in this paper
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome assembly; RNA-seq (NCBI SRA: PRJNA631442); metabolite profiling (ascorbic acid); WGCNA
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: One of six pathway/regeneration genes highlighted by coexpression modules positively associated with ascorbic acid content.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Dephosphorylates L-galactose-1-phosphate to produce L-galactose in the L-galactose ascorbate pathway.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms pathway intermediate leading directly to target product
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Higher in fruits and positively associated with fruit development; also high in stem
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Higher in fruits and positively associated with fruit development; also high in stem
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genome annotation; RNA-seq; WGCNA-metabolite correlation; ascorbic acid measurement
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to high ascorbic acid accumulation in guava fruits through the major L-galactose pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GPP was present in guava as part of a complete L-galactose pathway. 2. Expression of downstream ascorbate biosynthetic genes including GPP positively tracked ascorbic acid accumulation in fruit. 3. A GPP-related gene was included among six key WGCNA-associated genes for ascorbic acid accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key pathway component; not explicitly proven rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome assembly; RNA-seq (PRJNA631442); metabolite profiling; WGCNA

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes L-galactose to L-galactono-1,4-lactone, a direct precursor of ascorbic acid.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms immediate precursor of target product
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Fruit-enriched and positively correlated with developmental increase in ascorbic acid; also high in stem
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Fruit-enriched and positively correlated with developmental increase in ascorbic acid; also high in stem
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genome annotation; RNA-seq; WGCNA; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes ascorbic acid accumulation by producing the immediate precursor in the dominant biosynthetic pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GDH is encoded in the guava genome and belongs to the complete L-galactose pathway. 2. Downstream biosynthetic genes including GDH were positively correlated with fruit ascorbic acid content. 3. GDH-associated expression patterns support the L-galactose route as dominant in guava fruit.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: downstream key biosynthetic enzyme; not directly established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome assembly; RNA-seq (PRJNA631442); WGCNA; metabolite profiling

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes L-galactono-1,4-lactone to L-ascorbic acid, completing the L-galactose biosynthetic route.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: High in fruits and increasing with fruit development; also high in stem
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High in fruits and increasing with fruit development; also high in stem
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genome annotation; RNA-seq expression profiling; WGCNA association; ascorbic acid quantification
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Directly supports vitamin C biosynthesis in guava fruit through the terminal L-galactose-pathway reaction.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GLDH was identified as a downstream biosynthetic gene in guava. 2. Downstream biosynthesis enzymes showed positive correlation between expression and ascorbic acid content. 3. GLDH contributes to the evidence that the L-galactose pathway is the major source of ascorbic acid in guava fruit.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome assembly; RNA-seq (PRJNA631442); metabolite profiling; WGCNA

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces monodehydroascorbate back to ascorbic acid to regenerate the antioxidant pool.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Most MDHAR genes highly expressed in fruits, with exceptions for one member; positively associated with fruit development
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Most MDHAR genes highly expressed in fruits, with exceptions for one member; positively associated with fruit development
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative gene family analysis; RNA-seq; WGCNA correlation with ascorbic acid; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Major contributor to ascorbic acid regeneration in guava, more important than DHAR according to expression evidence.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MDHAR gene family is expanded in guava compared with related species. 2. Most MDHAR members are highly expressed in fruits and correlate with fruit development. 3. The study specifically concludes that MDHAR, rather than DHAR, contributes to ascorbic acid regeneration in guava.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control step in regeneration pathway suggested; not enzymatically quantified as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome assembly; comparative genomics; RNA-seq (PRJNA631442); WGCNA; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors highlight MDHAR over DHAR as the predominant regeneration component in guava.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes internal alpha-1,4-glucosidic linkages of starch to initiate starch degradation.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies sugars through degradation contributing to fruit softening
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Starch-derived soluble sugars linked to fruit softening
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Provides glucose/soluble sugars and contributes to ripening-associated softening
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Many AMY genes increase expression during fruit development/ripening
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Many AMY genes increase expression during fruit development/ripening
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative gene family analysis; RNA-seq; WGCNA correlation with starch content; starch quantification; positive selection analysis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes starch degradation during fruit ripening and is implicated as a main contributor to guava fruit softening.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AMY is one of the main expanded starch degradation gene families in guava. 2. Most starch degradation genes including AMY increased expression with fruit development while starch content decreased. 3. Two AMY genes (Pgu12819 and Pgu22300) were positively selected, and these PSGs increased during fruit ripening, supporting a role in fleshy-fruit softening.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: main degradation genes in guava; functionally prominent but not formally established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome assembly; RNA-seq (PRJNA631442); WGCNA; starch measurement; positive selection analysis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: AMY and BAM together account for 61.4% of starch degradation genes in guava.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Releases maltose units from the nonreducing ends of starch glucans during starch degradation.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: degrades storage starch to soluble sugars contributing to softening
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Starch-derived soluble sugars linked to fruit softening
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Sugar production during ripening and contribution to fruit softening
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Many BAM genes increase expression during fruit development/ripening
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Many BAM genes increase expression during fruit development/ripening
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative gene family analysis; RNA-seq; WGCNA-starch correlation; starch quantification
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely major driver of starch degradation associated with ripening and fruit softening in guava.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BAM is one of the two main starch degradation gene families expanded in guava. 2. Starch degradation genes including BAM generally showed increased expression during fruit development. 3. Their expression pattern inversely tracked starch content, supporting a role in fruit softening.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: main degradation genes in guava; not directly proven rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome assembly; RNA-seq (PRJNA631442); WGCNA; starch measurement
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Guava has more BAM members than surveyed comparison species.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: produces soluble sugars from starch breakdown contributing to softening/ripening metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Detected in genome; starch degradation genes broadly increase with fruit development
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected in genome; starch degradation genes broadly increase with fruit development
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genome annotation; comparative gene family analysis; RNA-seq expression profiling
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports starch breakdown pathway associated with guava fruit softening.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AGL genes were identified in guava as additional key starch degradation enzymes. 2. These genes were absent from E. grandis and P. granatum according to this analysis. 3. Their presence supports an enriched starch degradation capacity in guava fruits.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Highlighted as one of the additional key starch degradation enzymes not found in E. grandis or P. granatum in this survey.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supports starch degradation flux contributing to fruit softening
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Detected in guava starch degradation pathway; many pathway genes rise during fruit development
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected in guava starch degradation pathway; many pathway genes rise during fruit development
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to the enhanced starch degradation capacity linked to guava fruit softening.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DPE genes were identified in guava starch degradation pathways. 2. They were reported absent in E. grandis and P. granatum in the authors' comparison. 3. Their presence supports a richer starch-degrading toolkit in guava.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: enables starch degradation contributing to softening
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Detected in guava; starch degradation genes generally increase through fruit development
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected in guava; starch degradation genes generally increase through fruit development
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports the starch degradation pathway associated with fruit softening.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ISA was identified as an additional key starch degradation enzyme in guava. 2. ISA was not found in E. grandis or P. granatum in the comparison used here. 3. This supports strong starch degradation capacity in guava fruits.
